1: wayhî bayyāmîm hāhēm ûmeleḵ ʾên bəyiśrāʾēl wayhî ʾîš lēwî gār bəyarktê har-ʾeprayim wayyiqqaḥ-lô ʾiššāh pîlegeš mibêt leḥem yəhûḏāh — 'It came to pass in those days when there was no king in Israel that a certain Levite was sojourning (gār) in the remote parts of the hill country of Ephraim, and he took a concubine (pîlegeš) from Bethlehem in Judah.' The no-king formula sets the moral context. pîlegeš (H6370, concubine — a secondary wife with sexual rights but no inheritance claims; the word may be related to Akkadian pilakku, spindle-woman) — her status is ambiguous legally: she has the intimacy of a wife without full covenant protection. gār (H1481, to sojourn — to live in a place without full citizenship) — the Levite is himself a sojourner, dependent on hospitality, without territorial roots; his social vulnerability mirrors hers.
2: wattizneh ʿālāyw pîlagšô wattellex mēʾittô ʾel-bêt ʾāḇîhā ʾel-bêt leḥem yəhûḏāh — 'His concubine played the harlot against him (wattizneh) and went from him to her father's house in Bethlehem in Judah.' zānāh (H2181, to play the harlot/be unfaithful) — the verb can mean literal prostitution, infidelity in marriage, or in a softer reading simply 'became angry with him and left.' The LXX reads 'she was angry with him' — some scholars prefer this softer sense given his later tender retrieval of her (v3: 'to speak to her heart,' a covenant love idiom). The four-month separation (yāmîm ʾarbaʿāh ḥodāšîm) implies an extended estrangement, not a one-night departure.
3: wayyāqom ʾîšāh wayyelek ʾaḥărêhā lədabbēr ʿal-libbāh — 'Her husband arose and went after her to speak to her heart.' ḏibbēr ʿal-lēḇ (H1696 H3820, to speak to/upon the heart) is the OT idiom for words of tender comfort and persuasion — used of Isaac comforting Rebekah (Genesis 24:67, LXX), of Shechem wooing Dinah (Genesis 34:3), of Boaz's kindness to Ruth (Ruth 2:13). The Levite goes to win back his alienated wife with words of covenant love. He brings a servant and two donkeys (ḥămôr) — the travel equipment of a man serious about the journey. The father-in-law's repeated hospitality that delays their departure sets up the tragedy that follows.
14: wayyaʿaḇrû wayyēlkû ûtāḇōʾ lāhem haššemeš ʾeṣel haggibəʿāh ʾăšer ləḇinyāmîn — 'They passed on and went, and the sun set on them near Gibeah, which belongs to Benjamin.' The geographical detail is crucial: they chose Gibeah of Benjamin (giḇʿāh ʾăšer ləḇinyāmîn) over Jebus (Jerusalem, still a non-Israelite city). The servant had suggested Jebus; the Levite refused to enter a foreign city, choosing Gibeah — Israelite, Benjaminite, presumably safe (v12 — 'we will not turn aside into a city of foreigners who are not of the sons of Israel'). The choice of the 'safer' Israelite city leads to the worse outcome: the horror at Gibeah will surpass anything a Canaanite city could have done.
15: wayyāsurrû šām lāḇôʾ lālûn bəgiḇʿāh wayyāḇōʾ wayyēšeḇ birḥôḇ hāʿîr wəʾên ʾîš mēʾasēp ʾôtām habbayətāh lālûn — 'They turned aside there to go in to spend the night at Gibeah. He went in and sat in the town square, but there was no one who took them into his house to spend the night.' rəḥôḇ (H7339, the open square/broad place at a city gate) — sitting in the city square was the standard way to signal that you needed hospitality. wəʾên ʾîš mēʾasēp (no man gathering them in) — the failure of hospitality is the first sign of Gibeah's moral collapse. In the ancient Near East, hospitality to travelers was a near-absolute obligation; its absence signals a community that has ceased to function by covenant norms.
20: wayyōʾmer hazzāqēn šālôm lāk raq kol-maḥsôrkā ʿālay raq bāreḥôḇ ʾal-tālan — 'The old man said: Peace be to you; I will take care of all your needs; only do not spend the night in the square.' The old man's warning — 'do not spend the night in the square' — reveals that he knows the danger. He is a sojourner too (gēr v16 — from Ephraim, not a native Benjaminite), which may be why he, unlike the locals, shows hospitality: he knows what it is to need it. His injunction encodes the same vulnerability assessment the spies gave about Laish (Judges 18:7 — dwelling secure, no deliverer nearby) but in an Israelite city.
22: hēmmāh mêṭîḇîm ʾet-libbām wəhinnēh ʾanšê hāʿîr ʾanšê bənê-ḇəliyyaʿal nāsabû ʾet-habbayit mitdappəqîm ʿal-haddālet — 'While they were making their hearts merry, the men of the city, men who were sons of Belial (bənê bəliyyaʿal), surrounded the house, beating on the door.' bənê bəliyyaʿal (H1121 H1100, sons of worthlessness/destruction — the term for those who have broken all moral constraints) is the same phrase used in Deuteronomy 13:13 for those who lead a city into idolatry and in 1 Samuel 2:12 for Eli's sons. nāsab (H5437, to surround/encircle) + mitdappəqîm ʿal-haddālet (beating on the door) — the scene directly parallels Genesis 19:4-9 where Sodom's men surrounded Lot's house. The echo is intentional: Gibeah of Benjamin has become Sodom. An Israelite city in the Promised Land reproduces the worst act of Canaan's condemned cities.
24: hinnēh bittî habbəṯûlāh wəpilagšēhû ʾôṣîʾāh nāʾ ʾôtām wəʿannû ʾôtām waaʿăśû lāhem haṭṭôḇ bəʿênêkem — 'Behold, here are my virgin daughter and his concubine. Let me bring them out. Violate them and do with them what is good in your eyes.' The host offers his daughter and the concubine to protect the male guest — an act that reflects the extreme ancient Near Eastern hospitality code (protecting the guest at all costs) but violates the equally fundamental code of protection of women. ʿinnāh (H6031, to afflict/violate/humble — used for sexual violation in Genesis 34:2 Dinah; 2 Samuel 13:12 Tamar) — the offer to 'violate' them with this word makes the proposed act explicit. haṭṭôḇ bəʿênêkem (what is good in your eyes) — echoes Judges' refrain: the crowd will do what is right in their own eyes, the ultimate collapse of covenant standards.
25: wəlōʾ ʾāḇû hāʾănāšîm lišmōaʿ lô wayyaḥăzēq hāʾîš bəpilagšô wayyōṣēʾ ʾălêhem haxûṣāh wayyēḏəʿû ʾôtāh wayyitʿallălû-ḇāh kol-hallaylāh ʿaḏ-habbōqer — 'But the men would not listen to him. So the man seized his concubine and thrust her out to them outside, and they knew her and abused her all night until the morning.' wayyaḥăzēq (H2388, he seized/grabbed — the verb of force) — the Levite's action is brutal: he seizes (ḥāzaq) his concubine, the one he came to retrieve with tender words, and thrusts her to the mob. wayyēḏəʿû ʾôtāh (H3045, they knew her — the same sexual euphemism as Genesis 4:1 and Genesis 19:5) — gang rape continuing all night. wayyitʿallălû-ḇāh (H5953, they abused/treated wickedly — from ʿālal, to act severely against) — the compound horror: the night-long assault destroys her.
28: wayyōʾmer ʾēlêhā qûmî wənēlēkāh wəʾên ʿōneh wayyiqqāḥêhā ʿal-haḥămôr wayyāqom hāʾîš wayyelek limqōmô — 'He said to her: Arise, let us be going. But there was no answer.' The brutal simplicity of the Levite's response to finding her at the threshold: 'Get up, let's go.' No grief expressed, no lamentation — then the most chilling detail in the chapter: wəʾên ʿōneh (there was no answer). She is dead at the threshold, her hands on the doorway (v27 — as if still reaching for protection she never received). His matter-of-fact loading of the body onto the donkey and journeying home is presented without comment — the narrator trusts the reader to feel the horror he does not narrate.
29: wayyāḇōʾ ʾel-bêtô wayyiqqaḥ ʾet-hammaʾăkelet wayyaḥăzēq bəpilagšô waynattəḥêhā laʿăṣāmêhā lišnêm ʿāśār nətāḥîm wayyəšallāḥêhā bəkol gəḇûl yiśrāʾēl — 'When he entered his house, he took a knife, seized his concubine, and cut her into twelve pieces, limb by limb, and sent her throughout all the territory of Israel.' maʾăkelet (H3979, knife/slaughtering knife — from ʾākal to eat; used for the sacrificial knife Abraham took to Moriah, Genesis 22:6,10) — the same instrument of ritual sacrifice becomes an instrument of political summons. nātaḥ (H5408, to cut up/divide in pieces — the word for dismembering a sacrificial animal, Leviticus 1:6; Exodus 29:17) — the cutting is described with the vocabulary of sacrifice. The twelve pieces correspond to the twelve tribes — a territorial summons, sending to every boundary (gəḇûl) of Israel. The act mirrors the cutting of the covenant animal (Genesis 15:10,17; Jeremiah 34:18-19) — broken covenant requires a response.
30: wayhî kol-hārōʾeh wəʾāmar lōʾ-nihtəyətāh wəlōʾ nirʾətāh kāzōʾt lemibbennê yiśrāʾēl miyyôm ʿălôt bənê-yiśrāʾēl mēʾereṣ miṣrayim ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh śîmû-lākem ʿālêhā ʿūṣû ûḏabbērû — 'Everyone who saw it said: Nothing like this has happened or been seen from the day the sons of Israel came up from the land of Egypt until this day. Set your heart on it, take counsel, and speak.' lōʾ-nihtəyəṯāh wəlōʾ nirʾəṯāh kāzōʾt (nothing like this has been done or seen) — the hyperbole of moral horror: this is the worst thing that has happened since the Exodus, which is the founding event of the nation's existence. The repetition of nihtəyəṯāh / nirʾəṯāh (happened/seen) emphasizes unprecedented evil. The call to 'set your heart on it, take counsel, and speak' (śîmû... ʿūṣû... ḏabbərû) is a summons to corporate covenant response — what Israel does next will determine whether it can still function as YHWH's covenant people.
4: wayyaḥăzεq-bô ḥōtên ʾet-ḥātānô wayyēšeḇ ʾittô šəlōšet yāmîm wayyōʾklû wayyišt û wayyālînû šām — 'His father-in-law, the girl's father, urged him and he stayed with him three days; they ate and drank and lodged there.' The father-in-law's repeated delays (vv4-9 form a five-day accumulation) create the literary mechanism of fatal postponement. wayyaḥăzεq (H2388, he urged/strengthened) — the same root as ḥāzāq (strong) in Judges 18:26; here it is the pressure of hospitality rather than armed force. The hospitable father-in-law delays the Levite past safety; well-meaning social obligation becomes the proximate cause of catastrophe.
5: wayəhî bayyôm hārəḇîʿî wayyaškîmû ḇabbōqer wayyāqom lāleket wayyōʾmer ʾăḇî hannáʿărah ʾel-ḥātānô sāʿăḏāh nāʾ libbəkā pittāt-leḥem wəʾaḥar tēlēkû — 'On the fourth day they rose early in the morning and he prepared to leave. But the girl's father said to his son-in-law: Please sustain your heart with a morsel of bread, and afterwards you will go.' sāʿăḏāh libbəkā (H5582 H3820, sustain your heart) — the hospitality idiom for strengthening through food (cf. Genesis 18:5; Psalms 104:15). The repeated offer is genuine care, but the delay accumulates: each 'just one more day' pushes the departure later. The narrative's irony is that this warmth produces cold exposure.
6: wayyēšəḇû wayyōʾklû šənêhem yaḥdāw wayyišt û wayyōʾmer ʾăḇî hannáʿărah ʾel-hāʾîš hôʾel-nāʾ wəlîn wəyîṭaḇ libbeka — 'So they sat down and the two ate together and drank. Then the girl's father said to the man: Please be willing to spend the night and let your heart be glad.' wayyîṭaḇ lēḇ (heart be glad) — the refrain from Judges 18:20 (the hireling Levite's glad heart). The narrator's reuse of this phrase in a hospitality context that will lead to tragedy suggests a pattern: gladness in Judges is proleptic irony. The 'merry heart' of chapter 19 precedes the worst atrocity in the book.
7: wayyāqom hāʾîš lālaket wayyipṣar-bô ḥōtnô wayyāšāḇ wayyālen šām — 'The man rose to leave, and his father-in-law urged him, so he lodged there again.' pāṣar (H6484, to press/urge insistently) — a strong verb of social compulsion; it describes urgency beyond mere invitation. The brevity of the verse within the five-day accumulation highlights the relentlessness of the delay: the man rises, is pressed, relents. Agency is repeatedly yielded to hospitality convention. By the fifth day (v8), he will have lost the daylight window that could have saved his concubine.
8: wayyaškēm bayyôm haḥămîšî lālaket wayyōʾmer ʾăḇî hannáʿărah sāʿăḏāh-nāʾ libbeka wayyitmahmaḥû ʿaḏ nəṭôt hayyôm wayyōʾklû šənêhem — 'He rose early on the fifth day to leave. The girl's father said: Please sustain yourself. They lingered (wayyitmahmaḥû) until the day declined and the two ate.' māhah (H4102, to linger, delay, tarry) — the reflexive Hitpoel indicates mutual, self-imposed delay. The word appears in Genesis 19:16 for Lot lingering when the angels urged him to flee Sodom — here, five days before the Sodom-parallel atrocity at Gibeah. The narrator's intertextual signal is unmistakable: the man who should flee is lingering, just as Lot did.
9: wayyāqom hāʾîš lālaket hûʾ ûpilagšô wənáʿărô wayyōʾmer lô ḥōtəno ʾăḇî hannáʿărah hinnēh nāʾ rāpāh hayyôm liṣrōb hāʿereḇ lînû-nāʾ hinnēh ḥănôt hayyôm lîn pōh wəyîṭaḇ libbeka wəhiškamtem māḥār lədarkəkem ûhālaktā ləʾohāleḵā — 'The man rose to go — he, his concubine, and his servant — and his father-in-law, the girl's father, said to him: Behold, the day weakens toward evening. Please spend the night; the day is declining; lodge here and let your heart be glad, and tomorrow rise early for your journey, and go to your tent.' rāpāh hayyôm liṣrōb hāʿereḇ (the day grows weak toward evening) — the departing daylight is both literal and symbolic: after this point, darkness will prevail. The father-in-law's final offer is the decisive missed window: had the man departed days earlier, he would never have been in Benjamin at nightfall.
10: wəlōʾ-ʾāḇāh hāʾîš lālûn wayyāqom wayyēlek wayyāḇōʾ ʿaḏ-nokkaḥ Yəḇûs hîʾ Yərûšālaim ûʿimmô ṣemed ḥămōrîm ḥăḇûšîm wəpilagšô ʿimmô — 'But the man was not willing to spend the night. He rose and departed and came opposite Jebus — that is Jerusalem — with his pair of saddled donkeys, and his concubine was with him.' The parenthetical note hîʾ Yərûšālaim (that is Jerusalem) is a crucial temporal marker: at the time of this story, Jerusalem was still the Jebusite city Yəḇûs; David's capture of it is approximately 200 years away (2 Samuel 5:6-9). The narrator explains the city's identity to readers who know it as Jerusalem but must understand it was then pagan territory — which makes the refusal of v11-12 tragically ironic.
11: wəhēm ʿim-Yəḇûs wəhayyôm rāḏ meʾōd wayyōʾmer hannáʿar ʾel-ʾădōnāyw lēkāh-nāʾ wənāsûrāh ʾel-ʿîr-hayyəḇûsî-hazzōʾt wənālîn bāh — 'They were near Jebus and the day was very far spent. The servant said to his master: Please come, let us turn aside into this city of the Jebusites and lodge in it.' The servant's suggestion is strategically sound: shelter before nightfall. The master's refusal (v12) is based on ethnic/religious prejudice — the Jebusite city is foreign, therefore unsafe. The narrative's devastating reversal: the pagan city they bypassed would have been safer than the Israelite city they chose. The refusal of the alien becomes the acceptance of the worse.
12: wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw ʾădōnāyw lōʾ nāsûr ʾel-ʿîr nokrî ʾăšer lōʾ-mibbnê yiśrāʾēl hēnnāh wəʿāḇarnû ʿaḏ-Giḇʿāh — 'His master said to him: We will not turn aside to a city of foreigners who are not from the sons of Israel. We will pass over to Gibeah.' nokrî (H5237, foreign, belonging to another people) — the decisive word of refusal. The master expects covenant solidarity from a city of Israel's own tribe; the reader knows what will happen. This verse is the hinge of the narrative: the master chooses the known (Israelite, covenant) over the unknown (Jebusite, foreign), but covenant identity does not guarantee covenant behavior. Gibeah of Benjamin will prove less hospitable than any Canaanite city.
13: wayyōʾmer lənaʿărô lēkāh wəniqrəḇāh ʾeḥad hammaqōmôt wəlannû ḇeGiḇʿāh ʾô ḇārāmāh — 'He said to his servant: Come, let us draw near to one of the places and lodge in Gibeah or in Ramah.' Gibeah (Giḇʿāh) is the city of the hill country of Benjamin — the same city that will become Saul's royal residence (1 Samuel 10:26; Judges 15:34). The narrator's use of this name carries forward irony: the city of Israel's first king is first introduced as the site of Israel's most heinous atrocity. Ramah, the alternative, is Samuel's home (1 Samuel 1:19) — also a city of religious significance. Both options point forward to 1 Samuel's moral geography.
16: wəhinnēh ʾîš-zaqqēn bāʾ min-maʿăśēhû min-haśśādeh bāʿereḇ wəhāʾîš mihar-ʾEprayim wəhûʾ gār bəGiḇʿāh wəʾanšê hammāqôm bənê yəmînî — 'And behold, an old man was coming from his work in the field at evening; the man was from the hill country of Ephraim, and he was sojourning in Gibeah, while the men of the place were Benjaminites.' The old man's triple identification — Ephraimite, sojourner, old — marks him as the structural counterpart to Lot in Gen 19: the righteous alien in a wicked city who alone extends hospitality. zāqēn (H2205, elder/old) carries judicial and moral authority; the oldest person in the scene acts most humanely. The Benjaminite natives fail covenant obligation; the Ephraimite sojourner fulfills it.
17: wayyiśśāʾ ʿênāyw wayyarʾ ʾet-hāʾîš hāʾōrēaḥ bireḥôḇ hāʿîr wayyōʾmer hāzāqēn ʾānāh tēlēk ûmēʾayin tāḇôʾ — 'He lifted his eyes and saw the wayfaring man in the town square. The old man said: Where are you going, and where do you come from?' ʾōrēaḥ (H732, traveler, wayfarer — from ʾāraḥ, to travel) — the standard term for the stranger with a claim on communal hospitality (cf. the Didache's later use of parepidemos, stranger). The town square (reḥôḇ) was the expected place of hospitality-extension; that no one else approached all evening (v15b) shows the community's complete failure of the obligation the old man now discharges alone.
18: wayyōʾmer ʾēlāyw ʾōḇərîm ʾănaḥnû mibêt-leḥem yəhûḏāh ʾad-yarkətê har-ʾEprayim miššām ʾānōkî wāʾēlek ʿaḏ-bêt-leḥem yəhûḏāh wəʾet-bêt YHWH ʾānî hōlēk wəʾên ʾîš mēʾassēp ʾôtî habbayitāh — 'He said: We are passing from Bethlehem in Judah to the far reaches of the hill country of Ephraim; I am from there, and I went to Bethlehem in Judah. I am going to the house of YHWH, and no one receives me into his house.' The Levite reveals his itinerary: he is heading to the house of YHWH at Shiloh (cf. Judges 18:31). The stark contrast drives the theological indictment: a Levite going to the house of God cannot find a house of God's people in an Israelite city. The city of Benjamin has no hospitality for those who serve YHWH's house.
19: wəgam-teben gam-mispôʾ yēš laḥămōrênû wəgam-leḥem weyayin yēš-lî wəliʾāmāteka wəlannáʿar ʿim-ʿăḇādêkā ʾên maḥsôr kol-dāḇār — 'Yet there is both straw and fodder for our donkeys, and also bread and wine for me and your female servant and the young man with your servants — there is no lack of anything.' The Levite's careful enumeration of his self-sufficiency mirrors the Danite scouts' description of Laish: ʾên maḥsôr kol-dāḇār (no lack of anything, Judges 18:10). Both uses emphasize complete provision — but where the Danites used this phrase to justify theft, the Levite uses it to assure his host he needs nothing but shelter. The irony is that what he cannot provide for himself is safety, and that is precisely what will be denied.
21: wayyiḇîʾēhû ʾel-bêtô wayyibəlōl laḥămōrîm wayyirḥăṣû raglêhem wayyōʾklû wayyishtû — 'He brought him into his house, gave fodder to the donkeys; they washed their feet and ate and drank.' The reception sequence — house, fodder for animals, foot-washing, food and drink — replicates Abraham's exemplary hospitality in Genesis 18:1-8 and Lot's in Genesis 19:2-3. The old man's hospitality is formally perfect; every element of the covenant obligation is fulfilled. The parallelism with Gen 19 is structural: Lot's perfect hospitality (Genesis 19:2-3) preceded Sodom's assault (Judges 19:4-9); the old man's perfect hospitality (Judges 19:21) will precede Gibeah's assault (19:22ff). The structural echo is the narrator's interpretive commentary: Gibeah = Sodom.
23: wayyēṣēʾ ʾălêhem bàʿal-habbayit wayyōʾmer ʾălêhem ʾal ʾaḥay ʾal-terrēʿû nāʾ ʾaḥărê ʾăšer-bāʾ hāʾîš hazzeh ʾel-bêtî ʾal-taʿăśû ʾet-hannəḇālāh hazzōʾt — 'The master of the house went out to them and said to them: No, my brothers, do not do evil; since this man has come into my house, do not commit this outrage.' nəḇālāh (H5039, outrage, folly, disgraceful act) — the same word Tamar uses of Amnon's rape (2 Samuel 13:12: 'do not do this outrage in Israel') and that recurs as the narrator's label for what happened (Judges 20:6). The host's appeal ʾaḥay (my brothers) directly echoes Lot's appeal in Genesis 19:7 — the verbal identity signals the narrator's explicit Sodom typology. What follows in Gibeah is Israel's Sodom-moment.
26: wattipal-petaḥ bêt-hāʾîš ʾăšer ʾădōnêhā šām ʿad-hāʾôr — 'She fell at the door of the man's house where her lord was, until the light.' The concubine's fall (nāpal, H5307) at the threshold — the boundary between the house (safety) and the outside (death) — is the chapter's most harrowing image. She reached the door; she could not enter. Whether she was already dead when the Levite found her (v28) or died from his indifference is deliberately ambiguous. The threshold is the liminal space between life and death; she collapsed on it. ʿaḏ-hāʾôr (until the light) — she lasted through the night, which makes the Levite's callous departure-order ('arise, let us go') more disturbing.
27: wayyāqom ʾădōnêhā babbōqer wayyiptaḥ daltôt habbayit wayyēṣēʾ lāleket lədarkô wəhinnēh hāʾiššāh pilagšô nōpelet petaḥ habbayit wəyādêhā ʿal-hassap — 'Her lord rose in the morning, opened the doors of the house, and went out to go on his way — and behold, the woman his concubine had fallen at the entrance of the house, with her hands on the threshold (hassap).' hassap (H5592, threshold, sill) — the detail of her hands on the threshold is the narrative's final image before the dismemberment: she died reaching for the door. The Levite's response is to open the door, step over (or around) her, and prepare to leave — then say 'arise, let us go' (v28) before confirming she is dead. The narrator offers no explicit condemnation; the image condemns itself.
